items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What is the focus of virtue ethics?
items.correct: On character and habits — becoming the kind of person who acts well, aiming at eudaimonia (flourishing) through practising virtues that lie between extremes.
items.misconception: Virtue ethics tells you which specific actions are right and wrong.
items.random_wrong: Virtue ethics is about following the Ten Commandments.
items.why: Virtue ethics asks 'what kind of person should I be?' rather than 'what should I do?'. Students try to extract action-rules from it, missing its character-based agent-focused nature.

items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: Is Plato's cave primarily an allegory about life after death?
items.correct: No — it is an allegory of education and knowledge: the ascent from shadows to sunlight depicts the mind's journey from sense-based opinion to knowledge of the Forms, culminating in the Form of the Good.
items.misconception: The allegory of the cave is mainly Plato's description of what happens to souls after death.
items.random_wrong: The allegory of the cave is a literal report of prisoners Plato met.
items.why: Plato glosses the allegory himself in Republic VII: the cave is the visible realm, the ascent is the soul's education. Reading it as afterlife doctrine confuses it with myths like Er.

items.q: Is Aristotle's Prime Mover the same as the Christian creator God?
items.correct: No — the Prime Mover does not create ex nihilo, know particulars, or providentially intervene; it eternally contemplates itself and moves the cosmos only as a final cause.
items.misconception: Aristotle's Prime Mover is identical to the Christian God who creates and loves the world.
items.random_wrong: Aristotle's Prime Mover is one of the Olympian gods.
items.why: Aquinas adapted Aristotle but had to add creation, knowledge and providence. Listing what the Prime Mover lacks compared with classical theism is a standard comparison question.

items.q: In Plato's cave, the sun stands for:
items.why: Correct answer: The Form of the Good. “The visible world” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The formal cause of a statue of Hermes is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The figure of Hermes. “The bronze” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many causes does Aristotle distinguish in Physics II.3?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
